response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: After spending several hours daily on social media for years now, I have to say the fake content problem really gets under my skin. Working as a mechanic my whole career taught me one thing: you need to diagnose problems correctly or you will make things worse. Same principle applies here. I do not have patience for people spreading nonsense online, and frankly, I am tired of seeing my feed cluttered with obvious garbage that people somehow believe. When I see something suspicious, I apply the same troubleshooting approach I use in my shop. First, I check the source. Is this coming from someone credible or just some random account created last month? If a post seems designed to make me angry or worried, that is usually my first red flag. The fake stuff almost always tries to trigger strong emotions because that is how it spreads. I have learned to pause before sharing anything, even when it confirms what I already think. My approach is straightforward and maybe blunt, but it works. I look for multiple sources before believing health claims, political statements, or dramatic news. If I cannot verify something through at least two or three reliable outlets, I simply scroll past it. I also call out people in my circle when they share obvious fakes, which does not always make me popular, but somebody needs to say something. The platforms themselves are not doing enough, so we have to take responsibility. I worry about this constantly because misinformation can cause real harm, whether it is health advice or divisive political content. My Hindu beliefs remind me that truth and dharma matter, and spreading lies goes against that principle. I wish social media companies would crack down harder, but until they do, I stick to my systematic approach: verify sources, check emotions at the door, look for corroboration, and do not be afraid to challenge questionable content even if it ruffles feathers. It is exhausting sometimes, but necessary.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 64-year-old self-employed female mechanic with moderate conscientiousness, low agreeableness, and moderate neuroticism. Her vocational background suggests practical, hands-on problem-solving skills. Low openness indicates preference for traditional, proven methods. Low agreeableness suggests directness and willingness to confront others. Moderate neuroticism with several hours daily on social media indicates she likely experiences anxiety about online content. Her Hindu beliefs provide moral framework. Creative trait allows for practical problem-solving approaches. The tone should be direct, no-nonsense, practical, somewhat blunt, and grounded in real-world analogies from her mechanical work. She would approach fake content systematically like diagnosing mechanical problems, be willing to call out misinformation even if unpopular, express frustration with platforms and users, reference verification methods, and show concern stemming from her moderate neuroticism.
response_parsed.tone: direct, practical, no-nonsense, somewhat blunt, concerned, systematic
